steps.caption_md: Grouping `["eat", "tea", "tan", "ate", "nat", "bat"]`. For `"eat"` we count letters: `a:1, e:1, t:1`, written here as the key `a1e1t1`. No group has this key yet, so we start one.
`"tea"` has the exact same letter counts as `"eat"` — same `a:1, e:1, t:1` — even though the letters appear in a different order. What happens to `"tea"`?

predict.prompt_md: `"tea"` produces the same count key as `"eat"`. What does the algorithm do with it?

choices.label_md: Append `"tea"` to the existing group under key `a1e1t1`, alongside `"eat"`.
Start a brand-new group for `"tea"` because it's spelled differently from `"eat"`.
Sort `"tea"` and `"eat"` together into one alphabetical list before deciding.

predict.explanation_md: The key is built from letter counts, not from the letters' positions, so `"eat"` and `"tea"` collide on the same key and join the same group — that collision is the whole mechanism.

steps.caption_md: `"tan"` had a different count (`a:1, n:1, t:1`) and started its own group. Now `"ate"` counts to `a1e1t1` again — it joins the first group: `["eat", "tea", "ate"]`.
After one pass over all six words, three distinct count-keys have formed three groups — no pairwise comparisons were ever made, just one lookup-and-insert per word.
